summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is independent assortment, and how does it create genetic variation?

Independent assortment occurs during metaphase I when homologous chromosome pairs line up at the metaphase plate. The orientation of each pair is random — the maternal homolog can face either pole independently of every other pair. With 23 pairs in humans, this produces 2²³ (about 8.4 million) possible gamete combinations. Combined with crossing over, the genetic diversity is essentially limitless.

What is nondisjunction, and what can it cause?

Nondisjunction is the failure of homologous chromosomes (in meiosis I) or sister chromatids (in meiosis II) to separate properly. This produces gametes with one extra chromosome (n + 1) or one missing chromosome (n − 1). If these gametes are fertilized, the resulting offspring has aneuploidy (abnormal chromosome count) — such as trisomy 21 (Down syndrome, 47 chromosomes) or monosomy X (Turner syndrome, 45 chromosomes).
